metadata_checks.comments: Title, DOI, year, and review type match. The paper is broad climate-policy review, but it includes multiple direct carbon/fuel-tax acceptability studies, so tax scope is correct.

determinant_checks.raw_name_in_paper: people approved ... subsidies ... or expansion of public transportation. To a significantly lesser degree they supported two types of CO2 taxation
determinant_checks.comment: Directly supported; CO2 taxation is less accepted than subsidies/public transport in the Swiss comparison.

determinant_checks.raw_name_in_paper: were more likely to support a fuel tax if they believed the tax was going to reduce driving and exert a positive effect on climate change
determinant_checks.comment: Direct fuel-tax result; support rises when the tax is believed to cut driving and help climate.

determinant_checks.raw_name_in_paper: Perceived effectiveness best predicted people’s policy choices... more effective... than a carbon tax
determinant_checks.comment: Evidence is about general policy choice across environmental/carbon/geoengineering policies, not carbon-tax support specifically.
determinant_checks.suggested_correction: Remove or rephrase as general policy-effectiveness perception, not a tax-specific determinant.

determinant_checks.raw_name_in_paper: people may overestimate the costs of taxes and underestimate the ‘hidden’ costs of alternative policies
determinant_checks.comment: This is a general cost-misperception remark; the review does not test a tax-specific support effect here.
determinant_checks.suggested_correction: Remove or rephrase as general cost-perception context, not a direct carbon/fuel-tax determinant.

determinant_checks.raw_name_in_paper: distribution of costs is progressive... richer members... pay a larger share and potential revenues are redistributed
determinant_checks.comment: Supported, though framed broadly as domestic climate-policy burden-sharing and redistribution.

determinant_checks.raw_name_in_paper: support for fairness principles ‘equality’ and ‘need’... strongly positive impact on tax support
determinant_checks.comment: Direct fuel-tax fairness result; equality and need predict higher tax support.

determinant_checks.raw_name_in_paper: WTP... decrease if general taxpayers are assigned great responsibility... increase if industry and energy users are targeted
determinant_checks.comment: Directly references taxpayers versus industry/energy users in WTP for mitigation; sufficiently tax-relevant.

determinant_checks.raw_name_in_paper: acceptability of a carbon tax can vary between 68% and 48% depending on whether revenues are earmarked... or go directly to the general tax budget
determinant_checks.comment: Direct carbon-tax acceptability result; earmarking raises support relative to the general budget.

determinant_checks.raw_name_in_paper: Revenue recycling... reducing VAT or income tax — increased policy support
determinant_checks.comment: Direct gasoline-tax result; recycling revenues to VAT/income-tax cuts increases support.

determinant_checks.raw_name_in_paper: support a rise in fossil fuel taxes if revenues are earmarked... whereas without earmarking... prefers a decrease
determinant_checks.comment: Directly supported; earmarking fossil-fuel-tax revenues for environmental purposes increases support.

determinant_checks.raw_name_in_paper: policy support requires trust in government and politicians, as exemplified by the case of carbon taxation in Sweden and Norway
determinant_checks.comment: Directly supported; trust in government/politicians is linked to carbon-tax support.

determinant_checks.raw_name_in_paper: trust in environmental scientists and environmental organizations... trust in industry negatively associated with greater policy support
determinant_checks.comment: Policy support is general, not clearly tied to a carbon/fuel-tax outcome in the cited study.
determinant_checks.suggested_correction: Keep only if linked to a tax-specific result; otherwise exclude.

determinant_checks.raw_name_in_paper: support for a carbon tax falls when people receive information that only a minority of other citizens support the proposed tax
determinant_checks.comment: Directly supported; perceived majority support increases carbon-tax acceptability.

determinant_checks.raw_name_in_paper: a majority of participants favoured a carbon tax after deliberation
determinant_checks.comment: Directly supported; deliberation led a majority to favor a carbon tax.

determinant_checks.raw_name_in_paper: Republicans preferred to pay more... labelled as a carbon offset than... labelled as a carbon tax (no difference for Democrats)
determinant_checks.comment: Directly supported; the 'carbon tax' label reduces WTP for Republicans versus 'carbon offset'.

determinant_checks.raw_name_in_paper: improved public transport can reduce some public opposition to gasoline (petrol) taxes
determinant_checks.comment: Direct gasoline-tax result; public-transport alternatives reduce opposition.

missing_tax_determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Higher explicit policy costs reduce support
missing_tax_determinants.outcome_variable: Support for climate-policy taxes when explicit monthly costs are shown
missing_tax_determinants.evidence_quote: when different levels of monthly financial costs of a policy are explicitly described, public support for domestic as well as global climate policy goes down for higher levels of costs
missing_tax_determinants.comment: The review states that explicit monthly costs lower support, but the extraction omitted this direct cost-level determinant.

final_recommendation_summary: Keep the paper in the strict tax corpus. Most extracted determinants are supported, but three entries are not tax-specific enough, and the extraction misses the review's explicit higher-costs-negative-support determinant.
